era_context: The late Augustan age, with Augustus still living (he will die in 14 CE). The Roman Empire is at the height of its territorial extent and relative peace. In China, the Western Han dynasty has recently fallen (9 CE); Wang Mang's Xin dynasty is in its fourth year. In India, various kingdoms coexist; the Satavahana dynasty is rising. This is a year of stability in the Mediterranean and consolidation in Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse written records)
Interior of the Americas (no written records)

events.title: Augustus's final years and preparations for succession
events.description: Augustus, now in his 76th year, is in declining health. He has spent the previous years carefully preparing for his succession, ensuring that Tiberius is groomed for power and that the Senate and military are aligned. Augustus's authority remains supreme, but he is increasingly delegating administrative tasks. The succession is expected to be smooth, though there is some uncertainty about whether Tiberius will be accepted by the Senate and people.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's age and health in 13 CE are well documented. The preparations for succession are attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to 14-001 (Death of Augustus and accession of Tiberius)

events.title: Roman Senate grants Tiberius imperium and tribunician power
events.description: The Senate formally grants Tiberius imperium (military command) and tribunician power, making him effectively co-ruler with Augustus. This is a crucial step in ensuring the smooth transition of power. Tiberius is given command of the armies and authority over the provinces, though Augustus retains ultimate authority. This arrangement is designed to test Tiberius's readiness and to ensure that the military and Senate are comfortable with his leadership.
events.certainty_note: The granting of imperium and tribunician power to Tiberius is well attested, though the exact date is sometimes placed in 12 CE or 13 CE. The broader context is secure.
cross_references: Leads to 14-001 (Death of Augustus and accession of Tiberius)

events.title: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty faces mounting economic and natural crises
events.description: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty, now in its fourth year, is facing severe problems. The Yellow River is showing signs of instability, with reports of flooding and dike failures. Wang Mang's radical economic reforms, including land redistribution and currency manipulation, are proving unpopular and destabilizing. Bandit activity is increasing, and the government is struggling to maintain control. The dynasty is entering a period of crisis that will lead to its collapse within a decade.

sources.name: Hanshu (History of the Han), compiled by Ban Gu
Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled by Fan Ye

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records document the crises of Wang Mang's reign in detail. The chronology of natural disasters and economic problems is well established.

events.title: Expansion of Roman trade and cultural influence in the Mediterranean
events.description: Roman trade networks are expanding throughout the Mediterranean and into the Red Sea. Egyptian ports are becoming major hubs for trade with India and the East. Roman merchants are establishing trading posts and relationships with local rulers. This is part of the broader expansion of Roman economic power that will accelerate in the coming decades.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of Roman trade is well attested in archaeological evidence and in Strabo's account. The precise chronology of individual developments is less certain.
cross_references: Leads to 15-005 (Expansion of Roman trade to India)

events.title: Satavahana dynasty consolidates control of central India and Deccan
events.description: The Satavahana dynasty continues to consolidate its power in the Deccan and central India. The dynasty is issuing coins and inscriptions that attest to its authority and territorial control. Trade routes through Satavahana territory are becoming increasingly important for commerce between the Mediterranean and the Indian Ocean.
events.certainty_note: The Satavahana dynasty is well attested in numismatic and epigraphic evidence, but the precise chronology is debated. The broader context of consolidation is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative emphasizes Augustus's careful and deliberate preparation for succession, suggesting a smooth and orderly transition. However, some modern scholars argue that the succession was less certain than it appeared and that there were significant political tensions beneath the surface. The granting of imperium and tribunician power to Tiberius is sometimes portrayed as a sign of Augustus's confidence, but it may also reflect uncertainty about the succession and a desire to test Tiberius's readiness. The chronology of Wang Mang's crises is sometimes portrayed as a linear decline, but recent scholarship suggests that the situation was more complex, with periods of relative stability interspersed with crises.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 13 CE is rich for the Roman Empire, where the preparations for succession are well documented. Roman sources are detailed but written 80–200 years after the events. For China, the record is similarly detailed and reliable. For India and the broader Indian Ocean world, sources are sparse and primarily numismatic or archaeological. The Americas, sub-Saharan Africa, and northern Europe are absent from the written record.

graph_edges.note: Augustus's final years and declining health lead to his death in 14 CE
The granting of imperium and tribunician power to Tiberius prepares for his accession in 14 CE
The mounting crises of Wang Mang's Xin dynasty in 13 CE lead to worsening conditions in 15 CE
The expansion of Roman trade in 13 CE leads to further expansion to India in 15 CE
